segments.s053-g89: همه modifies روی and precedes it for emphasis: "the whole/entire surface." روی here means "surface" (not "face") and takes the following noun دریا as its complement: "the surface of the sea." شده is the past participle of شدن used resultatively — "has become/turned" — and قیرگون is a poetic adjective meaning "pitch-black." Word order is literal Persian: quantifier + noun (همه روی) + locative noun (دریا) + verbal predicate (شده) + predicate adjective (قیرگون).
segments.s053-g90: همه روی صحرا parallels the first clause: "the whole surface of the plain." Again شده expresses change/result "became" and the predicate is the nominal phrase جوی خون "a stream of blood." Note that in classical/poetic Persian the ezāfe linking جوی and خون is often omitted in meter, but the relation is still "stream of (the) blood." The whole clause thus reads: "became a stream of blood."
segments.s053-g17: مرا رفت باید بدین چاره زود is an idiomatic, somewhat archaic ordering meaning “I must go quickly by this means.” مرا functions as a dative/experiential phrase (“for me” / “I must”), while رفت باید is the fixed impersonal/modal sequence (“one must go” / “I must go”). بدین چاره زود is an adverbial prepositional phrase (“by this expedient, soon/quickly”) modifying the necessity expressed by باید رفت.
segments.s053-g18: رکاب و عنان را بباید بسود continues the same obligation: را marks the coordinated direct object (رکاب و عنان = “stirrup and reins”), بباید is the modal verb repeating the necessity (“one must / I must”), and بسود is an adverbial/complement meaning “to advantage / for profit.” Word order (object + را before verb) is a common poetic inversion; the whole means “one must (take/handle) the stirrup and reins to advantage.”
segments.s053-g19: اگر introduces a conditional clause (“if”). شاه is the subject “the king,” بیند is the present/subjunctive/indicative verb “sees”/“meets,” and ز جنگ‌آوران is a poetic prepositional phrase “from/among the warriors.” The clause therefore reads “If the king sees (them) among the warriors….”
segments.s053-g20: به کهتر سپارد سپاهی گران states the consequence: به کهتر is the indirect object phrase “to/into the lesser (one)” placed before the verb for emphasis, سپارد is the verb “he will entrust / assigns,” and سپاهی گران is the direct object “heavy / mighty troops.” Together: “he will entrust the heavy troops to the lesser (commander).”
segments.s053-g21: همان با درفش همایون شاه is a topicalized noun phrase: همان (“that very” / “the same”) + با درفش همایون (“with the auspicious/royal banner”) and شاه (“the king”) — the phrase highlights the king accompanied by his auspicious standard. Adjective-title همایون is an honorific attached to درفش (the banner).
segments.s053-g22: هم انگشتر تور با من به راه is a coordinated noun phrase adding another item: هم = “also,” انگشتر تور = “the ring of Tur” (proper-name sense of تور), با من = “with me,” and به راه = “on the road / on the journey.” There is no finite verb here; this phrase attaches to the previous clause to mean “also the ring of Tur goes with me on the road.”
segments.s053-g23: بباید کنون چاره‌ای ساختن is an impersonal necessity phrase: بباید = “it is necessary / one must,” کنون = “now,” چاره‌ای = “a (some) remedy / expedient” (indefinite object), and ساختن is the infinitive “to make / to devise.” Together: “Now one must devise a plan.”
segments.s053-g24: سپه را به حصن اندر انداختن is the purposive infinitival complement that finishes the thought: سپه را (the army, with را marking it as definite/object), به حصن اندر = “into/within a fortress,” and انداختن is the infinitive “to place / to set / to put (in).” The full idea is “to place the army into a fortress.” Note that ساختن ... انداختن form a single idea: devise a plan — namely, to put the army into the fortress.
segments.s053-g81: نه ... نه is a correlative negation meaning "neither ... nor." دژ (noun) and کشتی (noun) are the two negated items. بود is the past copular 'was' placed after the first noun; گفتی is a parenthetical particle/verb here (literally 'you said' or 'one would say'), inserted between the two parts to mark the comment. بر آب is a prepositional phrase 'on the water' modifying کشتی.
segments.s053-g82: یکی 'one' or 'a single' quantifies دود (smoke); دیدی is the simple past 'you saw' (second-person past, used narratively here). سراندر is a compound prepositional/adverbial phrase 'in/among/upon' and takes سحاب ('clouds') as its object, so سراندر سحاب = 'amid the clouds / up into the clouds.'
segments.s053-g83: درخشیدن is the infinitive/masdar 'shining, flashing' used as a noun phrase (subject) meaning 'the flashing/brightness of.' آتش and باد are coordinated by و, forming a compound subject ('the fire and the wind'). خاست is the past intransitive verb 'arose' or 'began' agreeing with that compound subject, so the clause means 'the fire's flashing and the wind arose.'
segments.s053-g84: خروش سواران is a genitive construction ('the clamor/roar of the riders'); و connects the next noun فریاد ('cry, shout'). خاست again is the past intransitive 'arose,' so the whole clause reads 'the riders' clamor and the cries arose.' Note the verb follows the nominal phrase as is typical in classical Persian narrative.
segments.s053-g85: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' خورشید تابان is a noun + adjective ('the shining/bright sun'). ز بالا is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from on high' or 'from above.' بگشت is a past-tense verb meaning 'turned' or 'returned' (third-person singular) and completes the temporal clause: 'when the bright sun turned from on high.'
segments.s053-g86: این چه ... چه ... structure is an exclamatory parallelism: چه ... و چه ... = 'what ... and what ...!' آن دژ نمود uses آن as a demonstrative 'that' and نمود as a past verb 'appeared/was seen,' so 'what a fortress that appeared!' The second half parallels the first: چه آن پهن دشت = 'and what a broad/wide plain!' The clauses are rhetorical/exclamatory rather than interrogative.
segments.s053-g87: بکشتند is the past plural 'they killed.' از ایشان marks the affected group 'of them' or 'from them.' فزون از شمار is an idiomatic adverbial phrase meaning 'greater than number' = 'more than could be numbered' or 'in countless numbers.' The whole clause: 'They killed of them more than could be counted.'
segments.s053-g88: همی is a frequentative/adverbial particle ('continually' / 'ever'), placed before the noun phrase دود از آتش ('smoke from the fire'). برآمد is the past intransitive verb 'rose/issued forth.' چو is the comparative/simile 'as/like' and قار is the final comparative image (an onomatopoeic or harsh-sound word); the clause reads 'continually smoke rose from the fire like (qar).'
segments.s053-g49: Imperative construction with the subject pronoun fronted for emphasis: تو (you) + با او (with him) frames the addressee's relation to the other person. به نیک و به بد is a paired prepositional phrase meaning "in good and in bad (times)"; the final یار باش is the imperative predicate "be a companion/ally." The verb باش is the imperative placed at the end, typical of Persian poetic word order.
segments.s053-g50: Two short imperatives in coordination: نگهبان دژ باش = "be (the) guardian of the fortress," where the noun phrase نگهبان دژ (guardian of the fortress) serves as predicative complement (the ezāfe is omitted in poetry). The second clause و بیدار باش repeats the imperative for parallel emphasis: "and be awake/alert."
segments.s053-g51: Temporal clause introduced by چو (when): چو دژبان چنین گفتها را شنید = "when the fortress-guard heard such words." گفتها here means "utterances/words" and is marked by the object marker را; شنید is a past tense verb at the clause end, as is normal in Persian.
segments.s053-g52: Direct statement: همان مهر انگشتری را بدید = "he saw that very seal of the ring." همان adds emphasis ('that very/just that'), مهر here means the seal (not 'love'), انگشتری is the ring (connected to مهر), را marks the definite object, and بدید is the poetic past tense "saw."
segments.s053-g53: Temporal phrase همان گه = "just then/at that moment," followed by the locative در دژ (in/at the fortress). گشادند is an archaic/poetic verbal form meaning "were opened" (instead of modern گشوده شد or گشودند), and باز complements it with the meaning 'open' — together describing the gates opening.
segments.s053-g54: Two short clauses compressed: بدید آشکارا = "he saw (it) clearly/evidently," where آشکارا is an adverb modifying بدید; ندانست راز = "he did not know the secret," with ن- negation prefixed to دانست (past tense of 'know') and راز as the direct object. The inversion and omission of pronouns/objects is typical of poetic compression.
segments.s053-g55: نگر is an imperative meaning "look" or "observe." تا introduces a purpose/result clause here ("so as to / to see"), so تا سخنگوی دهقان چه گفت = "to see what the farmer's spokesman said." سخنگوی دهقان is a compound noun phrase (the spokesman of the farmer) with the ezāfe omitted in poetry; چه گفت is the content clause 'what he said.'
segments.s053-g56: Relative/explanatory clause: که introduces "for/that": که راز دل آن دید = "for he saw the secret of that one's heart." آن refers back to the person in question. The final clause کو دل نهفت is archaic: کو functions as a relative/particles meaning 'who/which' here (an older usage), and نهفت is a past participial/verb meaning "had hidden/kept concealed"; together: "which the heart had hidden."
segments.s053-g9: کلانی (an adjective) qualifies دژش; word order is adjective + noun+possessive. دژش = دژ + enclitic possessive -ش (“his”), so literally “his great fortress.” باشد here is a subjunctive/optative‑type form (“should be / may be”), functioning as the copula linking the subject to آرامگاه (“resting‑place / tomb”).
segments.s053-g10: سزد‌گر is a conditional/adverbial expression meaning “if it is fitting / if it be meet.” برو is an imperative/hortative interjection (“go / let us go”); بربگیریم is a first‑person plural hortative/subjunctive (‘بر + بگیریم’ = “let us take up / seize”); راه is the object/goal “the road / way.” Together they form a hortatory clause: “if fitting, let us go and take the road.”
segments.s053-g11: که‌گر is the conditional conjunction “if / if ever.” حصن is the noun “fortress.” دریا is simile/metaphor “sea” (i.e. vast/overwhelming); شود is the present/subjunctive of شدن “become / turn into,” and جای اوی literally “his place” (جای + enclitic اوی = “his/its place”). The clause sets a hypothetical: “if the fortress becomes like a sea in his place…”
segments.s053-g12: کسی here functions as an indefinite with a negative force in context (“no one”). نگسلاند is a perfective/attemptive form of گسلانیدن/گسلاندن “loosen, detach”; in this construction it means “can/will not dislodge.” ز is the poetic preposition “from.” بن پای = “the root/base of the foot” = “foundation”; اوی is the enclitic pronoun “his/its.” The whole clause: “no one will loosen him from the base of his footing.”
segments.s053-g13: یکی is an indefinite/identifier “a certain / one.” جای دارد is the verbal phrase “has a place / there is a place” (دارد = ‘has’). سر اندر سحاب is a prepositional phrase with سر “head/top” + اندر (“in/within”) + سحاب (“clouds/firmament”), literally “its head is in the clouds” (i.e. extremely high).
segments.s053-g14: به چاره is an instrumental phrase “by/through a means/expedient” (به used instrumentally). برآورده is a past participle/perfective meaning “brought up / raised” (here passive/resultative). از قعر آب = “from the bottom/depths of the water” (از = from; قعر = depths; آب = water/sea). The phrase means “it was brought up from the sea’s depths by an expedient.”
segments.s053-g15: نهاده is a past participle of نهادن “placed / put.” ز هر چیز گنجی is a genitival/partitive phrase “a treasure from every thing” = “treasures of every kind” (ز = from/of; هر چیز = every thing; گنجی = treasure). به جای = “in that place / into the place.” The clause states that all kinds of treasures have been laid there.
segments.s053-g16: فگنده is a past participle/adjective meaning “thrown / cast (down/spread).” برو here functions poetically as a contracted prepositional phrase (بر + او) meaning “over it / upon it” in context. سایه = “shade / shadow”; پر modifies سایه as ‘feathered / full of feathers’ (پر همای = ‘of the Homa’s feathers’ or ‘feathered Homa’); همای is the mythic Homa‑bird. Altogether: “the Homa’s feathered shade has been cast over it.”
segments.s053-g25: A nominal phrase that names the actors and the time: من و گرد گرشاسپ = “I and (the) گرد of Garshasp.” وین تیره شب is a temporal/demonstrative phrase “this dark night.” Word order places the participants before the time phrase; گرد here is a noun element attached to گرشاسپ (see note).
segments.s053-g26: برین راز = 'with regard to this secret' (بر + demonstrative ین = 'on/over this'); بر باد = 'to the wind' (prepositional phrase). مگشای is a negative imperative/subjunctive (م‑ prefix + گشای < گشودن) meaning 'do not open/reveal'; لب ('lip') is the body-part noun that indicates how the secret would be revealed ('with the lips'). The object phrase (برین راز) appears before the verb, a common poetic order.
segments.s053-g27: چو is the temporal/conditional conjunction 'when/whenever'; روی هوا functions as 'the visage/complexion' or 'face of mood' (روی = face) and گشت is the past-tense verb 'became/turned.' The second چو introduces the simile 'چون آبنوس' = 'as/like ebony'; so the clause reads 'when the face became like ebony.'
segments.s053-g28: نهادند is the past, 3rd-person plural of نهادن 'they placed/set.' بر کوهه is a prepositional phrase 'upon the hill/mountain.' پیل کوس is a noun-noun sequence where پیل (elephant, i.e. huge) modifies کوس (war-drum) = 'an elephant-sized (huge) drum.' The verb governs the following locative phrase.
segments.s053-g29: A nominal phrase: همه نامداران پرخاشجوی = 'all the renowned/quarrelsome ones.' همه modifies the plural group (نامداران), and پرخاشجوی is an adjectival/epithet describing their temperament.
segments.s053-g30: ز خشکی به دریا is a directional phrase 'from the dry land to the sea' (ز = from, به = toward/into). نهادند is again past 3rd-plural 'they placed/put' used idiomatically with روی ('face') following to mean 'they turned their faces' or 'they directed themselves.' The object روی (face) completes the idiom 'نهادنِ روی' = 'to set/turn one's face.'
segments.s053-g31: سپه را marks 'the army' with the object marker را. به شیروی بسپرد is a past action 'he entrusted (بسپرد) to Shiruy' (به = to/for; شیروی is an epithet/name 'lion-like/Shiruy'). بسپرد is past tense 'he gave/entrusted.' و گفت links this clause to the reported speech that follows.
segments.s053-g32: که introduces reported speech 'that…'. من خویشتن را is emphatic 'I myself' with را marking the object. بخواهم is a present/subjunctive form meaning 'I will/wish/intend' (here expressing intention), and نهفت (from نهفتن/نهفت) functions as the verb 'to hide/conceal' in the subordinate clause: 'that I will hide myself.' The clause shows an embedded future/intended action in reported speech.
segments.s053-g1: The phrase begins with the prepositional phrase 'به سلم' (to Salm). 'آگهی رفت' is an impersonal/past expression meaning 'a report/information went' (رفت simple past of 'رفتن'). 'ازین رزمگاه' = 'از' + 'این' (here written 'ین') + 'رزمگاه' = 'from this battlefield' and follows the verb as the source phrase.
segments.s053-g2: 'وزان' is a fused conj.+demonstrative ('و' + 'از آن') meaning 'and from that'. 'تیرگی' is the noun 'darkness/gloom' (subject). 'کاندر' (archaic) = 'کِه اندر / کان در' meaning 'in/which in' or here functions as part of a locative sense: 'which/in that'. 'آمد' is simple past 'came'. 'به ماه' is a prepositional phrase 'up to the moon' — overall: 'and from that gloom it came up to (reached) the moon.'
segments.s053-g3: 'پس' is an introductory adverb here ('then/so'). 'پشتش' = 'پشت' + enclitic 'ش' = 'his/its back' or 'behind him/it'. 'اندر' = 'in/within/at'. 'یکی حصن' = 'one/a certain fortress' (word order adjective/numeral before noun) and 'بود' is the past copula 'was' placed at the end, giving the clause 'there was one fortress behind him.'
segments.s053-g4: 'برآورده' is a participial/past form meaning 'raised/upstanding' used with the noun 'سر' = 'head/top/summit' to describe the fortress' summit. 'تا' here means 'as far as / up to' and combines with 'به' ('to') + 'چرخ کبود' ('the blue/azure sky') forming the phrase 'its head raised up to the azure sky.' Note: 'برآورده' is an older/poetic verbal/adjectival form.
segments.s053-g5: 'چنان' is an adverb meaning 'such/so'. 'ساخت' is the past of 'ساختن' = 'he made/built'. 'کاید' (archaic) = 'a device/plot/cunning' — the direct object. 'بدان' here is 'ب' + 'دان' = 'به آن' = 'on/at that' (to that fortress); the phrase qualifies where the device was made. 'حصن باز' = 'حصن' (fortress) + 'باز' (here adjectival 'open') — word order places the modifying phrase after the noun in poetic diction.
segments.s053-g6: 'که' introduces a result/relative clause 'that...'. 'دارد' is present/past-aspect 'has/possesses' used idiomatically here about fortune. 'زمانه' is 'the time/the world/fortune' and takes the noun phrase 'نشیب و فراز' ('descent and ascent' = 'ups and downs') as what it has — literal word order 'time has descent and ascent' = 'the times have ups and downs.'
segments.s053-g7: 'هم' is emphatic/adverbial ('also/even'). 'این یک سخن' = 'this one saying/remark' (direct object). 'قارن' is the agent 'the companion/associate'. 'اندیشه کرد' is the compound past: the noun 'اندیشه' ('thought') plus 'کرد' (past of 'to do') forming the verbal expression 'pondered/considered.' The verb comes at the end as in Persian SOV order.
segments.s053-g8: 'که' introduces the content of the thought ('that ...'). 'برگاشتش' is transitive here meaning 'he took/interpreted it so' (برگرفت/برگاشتن sense) with the enclitic 'ش' = 'it' (referring back to 'این یک سخن'). The clause 'سلم روی از نبرد' reports the content: 'سلم' (proper name) + 'روی' = 'face' used in the expression 'turn one's face' and 'از نبرد' = 'from the battle' — together 'Salm had turned his face away from the battle' (i.e., withdrawn).
segments.s053-g33: شوم is the first‑person optative/subjunctive of شدن used here to express the speaker's intention ('I will / I shall go / I will make myself be'). سوی دژبان is a prepositional phrase 'toward the fortress‑warden' (سوی = toward; دژبان = fortress guard/warden). به پیغمبری uses the -i nominal form to indicate role or guise ('in the capacity of / posing as a prophet'), working adverbially with شوم.
segments.s053-g34: نمایم is the first‑person present/subjunctive of نمودن used here as 'I will show / present'. بدو is the clitic prepositional 'to him' (destination of the showing). مهر انگشتری is a compound object 'a seal (مهر) ring (انگشتری)' = 'a signet‑ring' (مهر = seal modifies انگشتری). The verb نمایم governs this object and the dative بدو.
segments.s053-g35: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. در دژ is 'inside the fortress' (در = in; دژ = fortress). شوم again is the first‑person optative/subjunctive 'I will be / I enter / I become', marking the speaker's action in that temporal clause. برفرازم is a poetic first‑person form meaning 'I will lift/raise aloft' (literally 'I exalt/raise up') and takes درفش ('standard/banner') as its object.
segments.s053-g36: درفشان here is an adjectival/participial notion 'shining, glittering' used with the causative کنم ('I will make / cause'). کنم is the first‑person singular of کردن meaning 'I will cause / make'. تیغ‌های بنفش ('the purple swords') is the direct object: 'I will cause the purple swords to shine/glitter.'
segments.s053-g37: شما is the addressee 'you (pl./polite)'. روی literally 'face' is used idiomatically with نهید to mean 'turn your faces' or 'face' a direction. یکسر is an adverb 'completely/entirely'. سوی دژ is the direction 'toward the fortress'. نهید is the classical present/subjunctive (used as an imperative) of نهادن/نهادن 'to set/put'—here 'set/turn' (i.e., 'direct your faces').
segments.s053-g38: چنانک 'so that / in such a way that' introduces the consequence/condition. اندر آید is a temporal/conditional clause 'when it/that comes' (آید = come/happen). دمید and دهید are jussive/imperative forms meaning 'blow' (دمیدن, here of trumpets or horns) and 'give' (دهید = give/produce the signal or shout); they are coordinated by و and instruct the addressees what to do when the moment arrives.
segments.s053-g39: سپه را uses the object/topic marker را to mark 'the army' as the thing being concerned. به نزدیک دریا is a locative phrase 'near the sea' (به = to/at; نزدیک = near; دریا = sea). بماند is third‑person singular of ماندن ('to remain') used narratively to mean 'was left / was made to remain / remained'—i.e., 'he left the army to remain near the sea.'
segments.s053-g40: به + name indicates under whose command or to whom the army was entrusted: به شیروی شیراوژن = 'to Shiruy Shirāvazhan' where شیروی is an epithet 'lion‑like/heroic' modifying the proper name شیراوژن. و خود براند: و = 'and'; خود 'himself' emphasizes the agent; براند is a narrative present of راندن ('to drive/ride/lead') meaning 'he rode away / set off himself.'
segments.s053-g57: مرا و تو را places the pronouns with the object/topic marker را after each (literary order: ‘for me and for you’). بندگی پیشه is a nominal phrase meaning “make servitude a trade/practice” (بندگی = servitude, پیشه = profession/practice). باد is the optative particle (‘may’) making the whole clause a wish: “May servitude be our practice.”
segments.s053-g58: ابا here is used as a noun (reluctance/abstinence) and combines with پیشه‌مان (‘our practice’, پیشه + possessive -مان). نیز is a coordinating adverb ‘also/too’. اندیشه followed by باد again uses باد as the optative particle, so ‘and let thought/consideration be ours’ or ‘may we also hold this thought.’
segments.s053-g59: به نیک و به بد is a paired prepositional phrase ‘in good and in bad’ (به = in; نیک/بد = good/bad). هر چه = ‘whatever’ (هر + چه), followed by شاید ‘it is possible/may happen’ to indicate possibility; بدن is ‘the body’ (what happens to the body). Together: ‘whatever may happen to the body, in good or bad.’
segments.s053-g60: بباید is a modal (‘must/ought to’). همی is a classical aspectual/adverbial particle that gives continuous or emphatic sense (‘always/continually’). داستان‌ها is the plural subject/object ‘the tales/stories’. زدَن here functions in the idiomatic sense ‘to set forth/tell’ (classical Persian uses زدن with narratives); the phrase means ‘one must continually tell these stories.’
segments.s053-g61: چو ... و چون ... is a parallel simile/construction meaning ‘as/like X and as/like Y’ (چو = like). دژدار and قارن are two person-titles/names; رزمجوی modifies قارن as ‘warrior/combatant’. This segment sets up a comparison: ‘such as the castellan and such as Qaren the warrior.’
segments.s053-g62: یکایک (‘one by one’/‘each’) is an adverbial; به روی = ‘toward the face’ (به = to/toward, روی = face). اندر آورده is a perfect/participial compound ‘having brought in/turned’, and the final روی repeats ‘face’ for emphasis — the idiom means ‘brought face to face’ or ‘turned their faces (toward one another)’. Word order is literary: adverb → prepositional phrase → participle → repeated noun for emphasis.
segments.s053-g63: یکی ... و یکی ... is the common paired phrase ‘one ... and another ...’ (یکی = one/a certain one). بدسگال and ساده‌دل are adjectival phrases describing those two persons (‘ill‑fated’ and ‘simple‑hearted’). The construction lists character types in balanced form.
segments.s053-g64: سپهبد is the subject/title (spahbed). The phrase به هر چاره آماده دل is inverted literary order: به هر چاره (‘for every expedient/plan’) + آماده (‘ready’) + دل (‘heart’), so ‘the spahbed had his heart ready for every device/plan.’ دل here is a postposed noun indicating the inner readiness (ready‑hearted).
segments.s053-g65: همی + past verb جست gives a continuous/persistent past action — ‘kept on searching’. The time phrase آن روز تا شب زمان follows the verb and literally means ‘from that day until the time of night’ (تا = ‘until’), so word order is verb → time-span instead of English’s subject-first.
segments.s053-g66: نه آگاه is a negated adjective phrase ‘not aware’. دژدار is the subject (the castellan) and the following از آن بدگمان is an adjectival/participial phrase ‘suspicious of that’ (از = ‘of/concerning’). The clause therefore reads roughly ‘the castellan, suspicious of that, was not aware.’
segments.s053-g67: به + indirect object (بیگانه) marks direction/recipient ‘to the stranger’. بر مهر خویشی is a prepositional phrase ‘upon affection/kinship’ (بر = ‘upon/on’, مهر = ‘affection’, خویشی = ‘kinship’). نهاد is past tense of نهادن ‘placed/put’, so the whole means ‘he placed/pledged kinship/affection to the stranger.’
segments.s053-g68: This short clause gives the consequence: از گزافه = ‘because of/through nonsense or boastful talk’ (از = ‘because of’). سر و دژ به باد is an idiomatic grouping ‘head and fortress to the wind’ i.e. ‘lost his head and his fortress’. بداد here functions as a past action ‘gave up/lost’ (the lexical list lacks the verbal sense, so index is -1).
segments.s053-g69: چو + clause = temporal ‘when’. شب روز شد is a poetic inversion ‘night became day’ (شد = ‘became’). قارن is the proper name/title (here the subject), followed by the epithet رزمخواه ‘eager for battle’ describing him.
segments.s053-g70: درفشی (the banner/standard) is the object; برافراخت is the past verb ‘he hoisted/raised’. چون گرد ماه is a simile ‘like the round moon’ (چون = ‘like’, گرد = ‘round’, ماه = ‘moon’) modifying the banner’s appearance.
segments.s053-g71: خروشید is an intransitive past ‘he roared/there was a roar’. بنمود is a paired action ‘and showed/displayed’; یک‌یک نشان means ‘the signs/standards one by one’ (یک‌یک = ‘one by one’), so the clause links an outcry followed by the sequential showing of standards.
segments.s053-g72: به introduces the recipients ‘to Shiruy’ (شیروی is here a name/epithet) and the coordinated phrase و گردان گردن کشان names the troops; گردان = ‘troop’ and گردن کشان is a participial descriptor literally ‘neck-stretching’ (i.e. with necks thrown back/proud). The preposition and coordination mark who saw the standards.
segments.s053-g73: چو is a subordinating conjunction 'when'. شیروی is the subject (a heroic name/adjective used as a person) and دید is the past-tense, 3rd-person singular verb 'saw; beheld'. آن درفش یلی is the direct object (آن 'that' + درفش 'banner' qualified by یلی 'valiant'), placed after the verb in this poetic word order.
segments.s053-g74: به کین روی بنهاد is a short clause meaning 'he set/turned his face toward vengeance': به + کین marks the direction/toward vengeance, روی 'face' is the noun that is 'set' (بنهاد, past of نهاد). با پردلی is an accompanying adverbial 'with courage' (با + پردلی). The verb بنهاد is past, 3rd-person singular, linking to the preceding subject.
segments.s053-g75: در حصن بگرفت is a locative + verb: در حصن 'in/into the fortress' precedes the verb for emphasis, and بگرفت is the past-tense 3rd-person singular of گرفتن 'he seized/took'. و اندر نهاد connects a second past verb (نهاد 'placed') with اندر 'into/in', leaving its object to follow in the next phrase.
segments.s053-g76: سران را is the definite object ('the chiefs' or 'their heads') marked by را. ز خون is an ablative/qualifying phrase 'with/from blood' (i.e., bloody). بر + سر + افسر ('on the helmet') gives the surface where they are placed, and the final نهاد is the past verb 'placed', taking سران را as its object — i.e. 'he placed the (bloody) heads of the chiefs upon helmets.' This is a terse, epic way to describe displaying severed heads.
segments.s053-g77: This is a zipper-like descriptive nominal phrase without a finite verb: به یک دست X به یک دست Y means 'with/at one hand X, with/at the other hand Y' — 'به یک دست' marks possession/position ('in one hand'). Here قارن and شیر are the two emblems/items placed in the two hands; the phrase modifies the subject (understood from the previous clause).
segments.s053-g78: به سر introduces a locative 'at the top' or 'on the head' (سر here = top/summit), followed by instruments/items گرز و تیغ 'mace and sword'. آتش و آب زیر places 'fire and water' beneath (زیر = under/beneath). The whole is a stacked description of the standard/ensemble: top: mace and sword; below: fire and water.
segments.s053-g79: چو ... رسید is a temporal clause 'when ... reached'. خورشید is the subject 'the sun'; بر introduces the surface/location 'on/upon'; تیغ گنبد is a poetic juxtaposition 'the dome on the sword' (an image of light hitting a blade), and رسید is the past 3rd-person 'reached/arrived'. The clause sets the time for what follows.
segments.s053-g80: نه ... نه ... is the correlative 'neither ... nor ...'. آیین دژ here means 'the fortress's custom/defense/established practice' (آیین + دژ), and پدید is the verb 'appeared/was seen'. Note: the word بد in this line is an archaic poetic form functioning as 'بود' (was) rather than the common adjective 'bad'; here it should be understood as the archaic copula 'was' — this is a poetic contraction rather than the literal sense 'bad'.
segments.s053-g41: بیامد is a third-person past (he came). چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. نزدیکی دژ is a poetically compact noun phrase 'near the fortress' (نزدیکی = nearness; دژ = fortress). رسید is another past verb (he reached/arrived) closing the temporal clause introduced by چو; the subject 'he' is shared by both past verbs.
segments.s053-g42: سخن گفت is a fixed expression meaning 'he spoke' (سخن = words/speech; گفت functions as 'said/spoke' in past). و links a second clause; دژدار is the subject of the second clause ('the castellan'). مهرش is the object 'his مهر' (here best read as 'his seal' or sign of authority) placed before the verb, and بدید is the past 'saw' — so the clause means 'the castellan saw his seal.'
segments.s053-g43: چنین گفت = 'he said thus/so' (چنین = thus; گفت = said). کز introduces a reported/relative clause 'that/from which' and here functions as 'that' introducing reported content. نزد تور آمدم = 'I came to the presence of Tur' with نزد + name meaning 'in the presence of (Tur)'; آمدم is first-person past 'I came.'
segments.s053-g44: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past 'he commanded/ordered.' تا introduces a temporal/durational complement here ('for' / 'for the space of'), زمان = 'time', دم here = 'a short breathing moment' (i.e. a brief rest), and زدم is the first-person past of زدن used with دم to mean 'I took a brief rest/took breath.' The clause is subordinate to the verb بفرمود (he ordered that I rest briefly).
segments.s053-g45: مرا گفت = 'he told me' (مرا = me with object marker را split in the line; گفت = said/told). شو is an imperative 'become' / here idiomatically 'go' or 'be and do' as part of the command. پیش دژبان = 'before/in the presence of the castellan' (پیش = in front of/in the presence of; دژبان = castellan/fortress-warden). بگوی is an imperative/subjunctive form 'say/tell' directed to the addressee; the whole segment is an order: 'Tell the castellan...' with the implied object 'me' receiving the command.
segments.s053-g46: که introduces the content of what to tell the castellan ('that...'). روز و شب is the adverbial pair 'day and night.' آرام و خوردن are two nouns coordinated ('rest/calm' and 'eating/food') that are objects of the following verb. مجوی is a negative present-subjunctive/imperative 'do not seek' (addressed indirectly in reported speech), so the clause means 'that day and night do not seek rest or food' → in natural English: 'that day and night he must not rest nor take food.'
segments.s053-g47: کز begins a clause ('that' / 'from which') linking to what follows. ایدر = 'from here' (adverbial). درفش = 'banner/standard', followed by the proper name منوچهر and the title شاه; together they form the nominal subject 'the banner of King Manuchehr.' The whole phrase sets up who is acting in the following verb-clause.
segments.s053-g48: سوی دژ is the directional phrase 'toward the fortress.' فرستد is the past verb 'sent' (he/it sent). همی is a small poetic particle often used for emphasis or continuous coloring (here strengthening the action) and is placed before the complement. با سپاه = 'with an army' (با = with; سپاه = army). Together: 'was sent toward the fortress with an army.'

sentences.s053-s45: The whole surface of the sea turned pitch-black; the whole surface of the plain became a stream of blood.
sentences.s053-s9: I must go at once by this expedient; I must take the stirrup and the reins to my advantage.
sentences.s053-s10: If the king sees (them) among the warriors, he will entrust the heavy troops to the lesser (commander).
sentences.s053-s11: That same king with the auspicious banner — and also the ring of Tur goes with me on the road.
sentences.s053-s12: Now one must devise a plan — namely, to put the army into a fortress.
sentences.s053-s41: There was neither fortress, one might say, nor any ship on the water; you saw a single plume of smoke up among the clouds.
sentences.s053-s42: Fire flashed and the wind arose; the riders' clamour and the cries rose up.
sentences.s053-s43: When the bright sun turned from on high, what a fortress that was, and what a wide plain!
sentences.s053-s44: They slew far more of them than could be counted; continually smoke issued from the fire like a cry (qar).
sentences.s053-s25: Be his companion in good times and in bad; be the guardian of the fortress and remain alert.
sentences.s053-s26: When the fortress-guard heard such words, he saw that very seal of the ring.
sentences.s053-s27: Just then the fortress gates opened; he plainly saw it but did not know the secret.
sentences.s053-s28: Look to see what the farmer's spokesman said, for he had seen the secret of that man's heart, which the heart had hidden.
sentences.s053-s5: If his great fortress should be his resting‑place, then, if fitting, let us go and take up the road.
sentences.s053-s6: If even his fortress should become like the sea in his place, no one can unloose him from the base of its footing.
sentences.s053-s7: One (place) lifts its head into the clouds; by an expedient it was raised up from the bottom of the water.
sentences.s053-s8: He has placed treasures of every kind in that place; the Homa's feathered shade has been cast over it.
sentences.s053-s13: This dark night, neither I nor Garshasp's host shall let our lips reveal this secret to the wind.
sentences.s053-s14: When the countenance changed and became as black as ebony, they set an elephant-sized war-drum upon the hill.
sentences.s053-s15: All the famed, quarrelsome champions turned their faces from the land toward the sea.
sentences.s053-s16: He entrusted the army to the lion-like Shiruy and said that he himself would hide.
sentences.s053-s1: News of this battlefield reached Salm, and that gloom came up to the moon.
sentences.s053-s2: Behind him there was a single fortress, its head raised up to the azure sky.
sentences.s053-s3: He made such a cunning device on that fortress that the fortunes of the age have their falls and rises.
sentences.s053-s4: That very remark the companion pondered — that Salm had turned his face away from the battle.
sentences.s053-s17: I will go to the fortress‑warden posing as a prophet and present him a signet‑ring.
sentences.s053-s18: When I am inside the fortress I will raise the standard aloft and make the purple swords glitter.
sentences.s053-s19: You (all) turn your faces wholly toward the fortress; and when the time comes, blow (the trumpets) and give the signal.
sentences.s053-s20: He left the army near the sea under Shiruy Shirāvazhan, and himself rode away.
sentences.s053-s29: May servitude be the practice of you and me; let reluctance be our practice as well, and let thought be ours.
sentences.s053-s30: In good and in bad, whatever may befall the body, one must continually tell these tales.
sentences.s053-s31: Like the castellan and like Qaren the warrior, each one in turn turned his face toward the other.
sentences.s053-s32: One ill‑fated and one simple‑hearted; the spahbed had his heart ready for every plan.
sentences.s053-s33: He kept searching from that day until nightfall; the castellan, suspicious of it, was not aware.
sentences.s053-s34: He pledged kinship/affection to the stranger; through vain boasting he lost head and fortress to the wind.
sentences.s053-s35: When night became day, Qaran the warlike raised a standard, round like the moon.
sentences.s053-s36: He roared and displayed the standards one by one to Shiruy and to the troop, whose necks were thrown back.
sentences.s053-s37: When Shiruy saw that valiant banner, he turned his face toward vengeance with courage.
sentences.s053-s38: He seized the fortress and put the chiefs inside; he set the chiefs' heads, bloody, upon helmets.
sentences.s053-s39: With a qaran in one hand and a lion in the other; at the top a mace and a sword, fire and water beneath.
sentences.s053-s40: When the sun reached the sword's dome, neither the fortress's custom remained nor did the fortress-guard appear.
sentences.s053-s21: He came; when he drew near the fortress, he reached it.
sentences.s053-s22: He said thus that I had come into Tur's presence, and he ordered that I rest for a short time.
sentences.s053-s23: He told me to go before the castellan and say that day and night he should seek neither rest nor food.
sentences.s053-s24: That from here the standard of King Manuchehr was sent toward the fortress with an army.
